questions.prompt: What does HTTP/3 solve that HTTP/2 didn't?

choices.text: TCP head-of-line blocking — HTTP/2 multiplexes streams at the HTTP layer but they all share one TCP connection; if any packet is lost, TCP holds back the entire stream until retransmission completes, blocking ALL multiplexed HTTP streams on that connection. HTTP/3 runs over QUIC (UDP-based), which has independent stream-level reliability — packet loss in stream A doesn't block stream B. Other HTTP/3 wins: faster handshake (TLS 1.3 integrated, 1-RTT typical, 0-RTT for resumption), connection migration (switching Wi-Fi to cellular doesn't drop because QUIC connections use an ID, not IP+port). For users on mobile networks or with intermittent connectivity, HTTP/3 measurably improves perceived performance. Adoption is broad in 2025-2026: Cloudflare, Google, Meta, Akamai, Fastly all support; major browsers default to HTTP/3 when available. Most pages should be on HTTP/3 via your CDN by now.

questions.explanation: HTTP/3 over QUIC eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking + faster handshake + connection migration.

questions.prompt: Your API returns 1MB of JSON for typical responses; clients on slow networks struggle. Easy fix?

choices.text: Enable response compression. Add `Content-Encoding: gzip` (or `br` for Brotli, modern and slightly better) to JSON responses. JSON typically compresses to ~10-20% of original size — that 1MB becomes 100-200KB, dramatically reducing transfer time on slow networks. Most HTTP servers and frameworks support compression with a single config flag (nginx `gzip on;`, Express `compression()` middleware, etc.). Clients send `Accept-Encoding: gzip, br` in requests; servers respond with compressed body. CDNs typically also compress at the edge automatically. Cost: tiny CPU overhead to compress/decompress; benefit: 5-10x throughput on slow connections + lower data costs for mobile users. One of the highest-ROI optimizations in web infrastructure. Combine with proper Cache-Control headers (so clients don't re-fetch unchanged data) and ETags (for conditional requests) — together these reduce most API bandwidth dramatically with minimal code change.

questions.explanation: Enable gzip / brotli compression. JSON typically compresses to 10-20% of original; trivial to add.
